utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay raymond it looks like you've been having some difficulty swallowing over for a period of time could you tell me like what's going on

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well i've been better for the last several weeks i've been noticing that it's been hard for me to swallow certain foods and i also have pain when i swallow down in my chest

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and when does it does it happen every time you eat

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it hurts not every time it hurts when i when i swallow most foods but it's really just the bigger pieces of food that seem like they're getting stuck

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and what do you mean by bigger pieces of food like what's your diet like

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well things have been stressed over the last couple of months so lacks a moving from the west coast of east coast so i've been drinking more eating things like pizza burgers i know it's not good but you know it's been pretty busy

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: wow that sounds kinda stressful like what are you moving for

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well i'm stressed because what i'm moving because you know i i do n't like the west goes so i i decided to move but you know it's just stressful

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: uh uh

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: because i do n't know how my dog is gon na handle the travel but i do n't wan na put them into the carbo portion of the plane we fly out of her really bad stories of dogs got in the wreck

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so are you thinking of driving

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i i think so i think i'm i think i'm gon na end up driving but that's still a a long trip

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah absolutely i can see how that would that would increase your stress but like with that have you lost any weight because of your symptoms

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i wish unfortunately i've gained some weight

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and do you have any other symptoms like abdominal pain nausea vomiting diarrhea

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sometime my belly hurts up here

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright so epigastric pain alright any blood in your stool or dark dark tarry stool

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: not that i noticed

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright so i'm gon na go ahead and do my physical exam i'll be calling up my findings as i run through it if you have any questions please let me know alright so with your vital signs your blood pressure looks pretty decent we have it like one thirty three over seventy so that's fine your heart rate looks good you do n't have a fever i do notice that in your chart it looks like you have gained you know about like ten pounds over the last month so i i do understand when you say that you've experienced some weight gain your you're satting pretty well your o2 sat is at a hundred percent so and then your breathing rate is pretty normal at nineteen so i'm gon na go ahead and do my mouth exam there are no obvious ulcers or evidence of thrush present tonsils are midline your neck i do n't appreciate any adenopathy no thyroid thyromegaly on your abdomen it is nondistended active bowel sounds so when i press here on that top part of your stomach does it hurt

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i did that hurts

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay pain to palpation of epigastric area how about now

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay negative murphy's sign no peritoneal signs no rebound your on examination of the lungs they sound clear to auscultation bilaterally i do n't see any rash no lesion no bruising your eyes seem equal and reactive to light so all of these things sound pretty decent so let's talk about like the results that i got for your i reviewed the results of your barium swallow and it showed that you have two areas of mild narrowing in the mid and lower portions of your esophagus that can be found in patients experiencing something called esophagitis so for your primary primary problem you have acute esophagitis i wan na go ahead and prescribe protonix it's forty milligrams you're gon na take that once a day you should take it the first thing in the morning i also wan na prescribe to you something called carafate you take one gram four times a day for one month that's just gon na help kind of coat your the in the lining of your esophagus and like your stomach so that you're again like not producing a whole lot of acid like your your pretty much your the acid in your stomach is getting where it does n't need to be and it's a bit too strong so we're gon na give your body time to do a reset i wan na schedule you for an upper endoscopy just to be sure we are n't missing anything else i encourage you to change your diet and decrease alcohol and caffeine i know that's gon na be pretty hard with the move but you know once especially once you're settled in it's gon na be very important for us to to like focus on like getting well and eating healthy so that you know like you can you can move about your day as best as you can and and enjoy your move i want you to consider like eating slowly and chewing your food more thoroughly so that you do n't have to deal with those big pieces i also want you to avoid citrus foods fruits and spicy foods until your symptoms have improved i wan na see you again next week for that endoscopy i know there was a lot of information do you have any questions

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i think that's all good

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright thank you so much for coming in

detail_list.summary: The patient presents with difficulty swallowing.
The doctor asks the patient to describe their symptoms.
The patient reports difficulty swallowing and chest pain for several weeks.
The doctor asks if the dysphagia occurs with every meal.
The patient reports that swallowing larger pieces of food causes them to feel stuck.
The doctor asks about the patient's current diet.
The patient reports recent stress due to moving and increased consumption of alcohol, pizza, and burgers.
The doctor asks if the patient has experienced any weight loss.
The patient denies weight loss and reports weight gain.
The doctor asks about other symptoms including abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea.
The patient reports epigastric pain.
The doctor asks if the patient has noticed dark or tarry stools.
The patient denies dark or tarry stools.
The doctor documents vital signs: BP 133/70, SpO2 100%, respiratory rate 19, and notes weight gain.
The doctor performs a physical exam noting no mouth ulcers, no thrush, midline tonsils, no adenopathy, no thyromegaly, and active bowel sounds.
The doctor asks if the patient feels pain during epigastric palpation.
The patient confirms pain upon epigastric palpation.
The doctor notes pain to palpation of the epigastric area.
The doctor notes negative Murphy's sign, no peritoneal signs, no rebound, clear lungs, no skin lesions, and reactive pupils.
The barium swallow study revealed two areas of mild narrowing in the mid and lower esophagus.
Acute esophagitis.
The doctor prescribes Protonix 40 mg daily in the morning.
The doctor prescribes Carafate 1 gram four times daily for one month.
The doctor recommends an upper endoscopy and a follow-up visit in one week.
The doctor advises dietary changes including decreasing alcohol and caffeine, avoiding citrus, fruits, and spicy foods, and eating slowly.
The doctor asks if the patient has any questions.
The patient has no further questions.
